Tennis Automated | ATP Fantasy | Week 4 Draw Update

Week 4 Cheat Sheet

Draw update for 20-26 April 2026. Week 4 stays centered on the Madrid ATP Masters 1000, with the official event window live from 22 April - 3 May, 2026. Now that the bracket is in focus, the fantasy edge shifts to quarter balance, path quality, Bonus Ball leverage, and which power-based clay profiles can actually convert this draw into wins.

Week Window 20-26 April
Slate Madrid only
Surface Clay altitude
Key Date Event starts: 22 Apr

Tournament Lens

Top Of Board

Madrid remains the full ATP Fantasy focus, and the draw update still starts with the biggest names in the PIF ATP Live Rankings. Jannik Sinner, Carlos Alcaraz, Novak Djokovic, Alexander Zverev, and Andrey Rublev all stay relevant because this event rewards players who can hold serve, shorten points, and pressure opponents early in rallies.

  • Best pure floor: Sinner
  • Best Madrid-specific ceiling: Alcaraz
  • Best veteran upside: Djokovic
  • Best altitude fit outside the top tier: Zverev

Fantasy Shape

With the official draw now live, lineup building should be more bracket-driven than reputation-driven. Madrid is still faster than a typical clay stop, so the best builds lean toward players with real round-by-round advancement equity, not just generic clay comfort. The key question is no longer who fits the conditions in theory, but whose section gives them room to turn that fit into a deep run.

  • Prioritize quarter and section clarity over name value alone.
  • Give extra weight to serve bonus potential in altitude.
  • Avoid overstacking one crowded draw pocket.
  • Use alternates to protect against volatile early-round paths.

What Matters This Week

ATP Fantasy still gives you a 100-credit budget, six starters, two alternates, and one Bonus Ball player who scores double. On a standalone ATP Masters 1000 slate, advancement points and efficient wins remain the cleanest path to a strong score.

With the draw in place, the biggest edge comes from identifying which elite names have the cleanest route, which mid-tier players landed in playable sections, and which popular picks now face too much early resistance. In Madrid, serve quality and first-strike control still matter more than at slower clay events, but bracket shape now decides which profiles are truly actionable.

+2 per ace +10 straight-sets win +20 upset win -2 per double fault -20 upset loss
Best Week 4 Angles
  • Anchor builds with one elite title favorite whose section looks manageable.
  • Treat Madrid as faster clay, not baseline-only clay.
  • Use live-ranking prices as a guide, then let the draw break ties.
  • Downgrade popular names if their quarter is overloaded early.

Draw Update Priorities

Best bracket reads

The strongest fantasy builds now come from players with both title equity and a route that does not force too many coin-flip matches too early. If a favorite avoided dangerous floaters and heavy-hitting early opposition, that matters immediately.

Best scoring environment

Madrid still boosts the value of aces, holds, and straight-sets wins more than a slower clay event would. Players who can protect serve and finish sets cleanly gain extra fantasy appeal once their path looks stable.

Best use of alternates

Alternates matter more after the draw because they let you cover volatile sections without fully committing starter spots. Use them on upside names whose path is playable but not fully trustworthy.

Best fade signal

If a player is expensive, popular, and sitting in a crowded section with multiple dangerous hitters, that is often enough to move off the play. Draw friction can erase even strong event fit.

Bonus Ball Board

1. Carlos Alcaraz

Alcaraz remains the most natural Madrid Bonus Ball candidate if his route is even reasonably clean. His event-specific ceiling is elite, and these conditions still amplify his ability to take control quickly.

2. Jannik Sinner

Sinner is still the safest elite Bonus Ball alternative. If you prefer stability over pure event-specific upside, he remains one of the strongest double-score anchors on the board.

3. Alexander Zverev

Zverev stays firmly in the Bonus Ball mix because Madrid can reward his serve and first-strike patterns in a big way. If his quarter is not overloaded, he becomes one of the best leverage captain-style plays.

Core Plays

Carlos Alcaraz

Still one of the clearest core plays because his Madrid fit and title-winning ceiling are obvious. If the draw did not hand him an immediate gauntlet, he belongs near the center of roster construction.

Jannik Sinner

Sinner remains a premium core option thanks to his elite weekly floor and reliable deep-run profile. He does not need the perfect section to stay relevant, though a cleaner route only strengthens the case.

Alexander Zverev

Zverev remains one of the strongest non-top-two core plays on this slate. In Madrid, his serve profile can create both safety and spike scoring if the bracket gives him room to settle in.

Value Targets

Andrey Rublev

Rublev stays on the value radar because Madrid is one of the clay events where his aggressive patterns can still scale. If his section is not packed with elite return pressure, he becomes much more attractive.

Ben Shelton

Shelton remains the kind of upside value who can matter more here than on slower clay. If the draw gives him a path without immediate top-tier returners, the ace scoring can make him a real lineup piece.

Joao Fonseca

Fonseca still fits the upside-value archetype when price lags behind threat level. In a draw where he is not immediately blocked by elite seeds, his attacking game can become fantasy relevant quickly.

Serve-first risers in open sections

This is still the week to scan for players whose ranking-based price has not fully caught up to current level. If they landed in a manageable pocket and can bank holds, they deserve serious consideration.

Trap List

Crowded-quarter stars

Even elite names can become inefficient if their quarter is loaded with dangerous early-round opponents. If the route is full of live hitters, the price can stop making sense.

Slow-clay specialists in bad sections

Madrid does not reward every clay profile equally. If a player depends on long rallies and also drew power opponents, the fantasy ceiling can flatten fast.

Popular names with workload or form uncertainty

Draw quality cannot fully erase physical or rhythm concerns. If a player enters with recent workload questions or unclear form, the downside remains real even in a decent section.

Draw update refreshed on 18 April 2026 using official ATP Madrid event timing, current ATP live-ranking context, and the live draw/scores hub. Focus lineup decisions on bracket shape, section difficulty, and Bonus Ball path quality.
